speaker: interviewer
text: This is March 16, 1995. It is an interview for the Shoah Foundation with Paula Lebovics. I am the interviewer, Donna Kanter. We are in Encino, California, in the County of Los Angeles in the United States of America. And the interview will be in English. Good. This is an interview with Paula Lebovics. Spelling is P as in Paul-- A-U-L-A, Lebovics, L-E-B as in boy-- O-V as in Victor-- I-C-S. Paula was born Pesa Balter.

speaker: interviewer
text: P-E-S-A, Balter, B as in boy-- A-L-T-E-R on September 25, 1933 in Ostrowiec, Poland. I'm the interviewer for the Shoah Foundation. My name is Donna Kanter, K-A-N-T-E-R. We are in Encino, California in the County of Los Angeles in the United States of America. Today's date is March 16, 1995. And Paula, would you tell me for us-- for the record, your-- your name and the age you are today.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Paula Lebovics, and I'm 61.

speaker: interviewer
text: May we begin with your childhood in Poland before the occupation, what your life was like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was-- I was born amidst a very large family. My-- my grandfather was quite well-to-do, and he built himself a big building. And in the back of the building in the courtyard, he built houses for each one of the children so-- so that there were a lot of children playing in the yard. We had our own little building. Of course, everything is relative. In those days, they were small little houses in the back. And I was the youngest one of six children, and my oldest brother was already 13 years old when I was born. So there were lots of children in the yard, and it was always very lively. And we were considered sort of well-to-do in a way because of my grandfather. But the whole family worked for my grandfather. My mother worked for him. My father worked for him. All my uncles worked for him. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it-- it was by the-- just before the war broke out, which made me like five years old still, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I had broken my arm. And my first taste of the war was during that time, just before the war broke out, when I had to set my arm, and I recall it very vividly. It was on a Friday, and they used to bathe the children outside like in a [POLISH]. What's called a [POLISH] is like a basin. And I was bathed for Shabbat, and-- and when my mother-- may she rest in peace-- when she touched me, I started screaming when she touched my arm, and nobody knew why. So naturally, we had to go on to make the Shabbat. And as-- as Saturday morning came, my arm became swollen. And my father, being a very, very religious man, he never-- he never-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what would that word be-- the-- like you'd desecrated the Shabbos. And he-- he took me to the hospital and they took some kind of X-rays, and they gave my-- my father a diagnosis that I had sclerosis of the bones. Well, it was Tisha B'Av in our family, which means it's a-- it's a-- Tisha B'Av is a very sad day when our temple was destroyed. So it was very sad in our home. After the Shabbat was over, my father took me in a-- in a-- cart to the next town, where, for the first time in my memory, I met my other grandfather and grandmother [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in-- in Opatow, which was a town not too far from us. I would say several kilometers from our-- from Ostrowiec. And I got a chance to meet my grandmother. She was a little tiny woman, even in my memory, because my grandfather was very, very tall. Even for today's generation, he would have been considered tall. And they all got around, the family, and decided that I should be taken to Warsaw. And it seems if, through a family, somebody that knew the doctor, who was also related, and they took further tests and-- and determined that it was only a break. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In the meantime, I forgot to tell you is that while the Shabbat was going on, my father took me to a feldsher. Now, a feldsher is-- is a practitioner. It's not a doctor. It's a practitioner. And he started to move my arm to try to settle it. And by doing so, he even made it worse. The-- the fracture became a break, and I was in terrible pain. The reason I'm telling this story is because my arm went on until the war broke out. It went on for quite a few months. It went on for-- it seemed like forever. Because in Warsaw, they set my arm straight, which was already starting to heal wrongly because time has elapsed. So my arm never got a chance to be set. And they put me in a cast, which they told me to keep on X amount of time. And I remember coming home. It was very uncomfortable, because it started itching, and it seemed like there were worms under the-- under the plaster. And in the meantime, I developed a very good weapon against the children, [LAUGHS] all the children. Because if they would come close to me, I would threaten them. I'm going to hit them with my-- with my cast. Well, it seemed that when they took off the plaster, my arm was not healed properly, and they had to go back. And that's when the war was already on. And I remember my father taking me to Warsaw when the war was on, and how the Germans didn't let us get into the-- into the-- it's either was a train or a bus or whatever it was. And they-- they-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they made us sit somewhere. I-- I don't remember, but I remember they were very stern. And this was just in the beginning, very beginning. It was still-- I'm pretty sure it was still 1939. And Ostrowiec was-- Poland was just invaded on-- on September 1. So this might have been at the very end of '39 or the beginning of '40. And we had a hard time coming back from Warsaw. We had to take a-- a horse and buggy to come home because they wouldn't let us on a train anymore because we were Jews. And that's the reason I told this story. My arm never did heal well. I still have a crooked arm, but it's good. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then next thing I remember is-- well, they let us stay in our house for approximately, I would say, for a year after the war started. They let us stay in our home. My grandfather built his building in a beautiful section of town, which the Germans had an eye on. And as soon as the proper time came, which I would say about a year later, they evacuated us. And by that time already, an open ghetto was formed in our town. By an open ghetto, what I mean is-- is-- is there were posters posted at certain places in our town, where Jews were not allowed to cross anymore. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] While I was still in my house, I remember little things with my cousins. I remember my-- little things that I passed on to my kids. I remember my cousin. I always thought she was very mean to me, because she was a little older, and she got a chance to go to school. And she used to come home, and-- and they had a garden. And they put a little short picket fence around it, and I wanted to go in. And she says, you can't smell my flowers. You're going to smell them all out. You're going to get-- those-- those little things I remember and how you recall later on this cousin unfortunately never made it through the war. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were in the open ghetto. I actually liked the open ghetto very much. They put us into one big-- big room at a section of town, which was around a big market. They called it a market in Europe. Most of the medieval towns had marketplaces. It's like a cobblestone area, which was a circle, and around this circle there were houses built. And that circle was quite large, because all the wagons from the neighboring farms used to come and sell their goods and-- and-- and practice. But naturally, when we were put in that ghetto no more carts came, and no more goods were being sold in that marketplace.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they put us in one of the rooms in one of the buildings around that place, and-- and we-- I had a chance to be with everybody together. And it seemed like there wasn't too much going on, so everybody was home, and I had the attention that I really needed, I guess.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was very nice. I got to sleep with my sisters, and I got to be with everybody all the time. I could sleep wherever I wanted, actually. The only bad thing, they really didn't want to sleep with them, because I-- I-- I was still wetting. I did that throughout the whole war. Later on, it-- it came back to me, because I was scared all the time, and I-- I hardly slept through a night without wetting. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] After-- after that ghetto, my brother found out-- my brother Herschel, who was my oldest brother-- he was 13 years older than me-- he found out about that they're going to have a selection. And he had planned this a long time already while we were still in our own home, which I found out later. They-- they build a bunker under a chicken farm in that open ghetto, and the whole family was put in there. He was a-- a Jewish policeman. He was 20 years old. And he put us all-- he-- into that-- into that bunker under the chicken coop, the whole family, whoever he could get to go under there. And we waited out the first selection. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] By the time-- also, I-- I forgot again. During the open ghetto time, there were a lot of hangings. And they were all done in that marketplace, which was right in front of our buildings all over. And they used-- it started out with the-- the heads of-- of Ostrowiec, which most of them were not even Jews. It started out with the hangings, and we all were invited to watch. We had to watch, in other words. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And also, a very important thing happened during that time is that my brother Jonathan, who is the second oldest, he ran away during the beginning-- very beginning of the war. He ran away to Litva. And in-- in doing so, he found out that they were-- Jews were being unkindly treated. He went to the Red Cross over in Litva, and he demanded to know how we are. So I recall when-- when the SS came into our house, and they took us all. They marched us, and they-- they made sure that we all dressed properly. And they took us to a photographer, and they had our picture taken. And I have a picture with our family in armbands, and I think my brother and my father were given jackets so that they looked presentable. And-- because when they marched us out, they didn't give us time to do anything, just. And that picture ended up in my brother's hand in Litva, who, in turn, took it with him, and it survived. And we all have copies of that picture. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Then afterwards, we got into the bunker, and the first selection took place. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] After the selection, my sisters went out to be selected, because they figured they were 16 and 18 at the time. And nobody's going to harm them, because they are strong, and they're young. They're going to go to work. But they never returned. I was told by people that watched that selection that they were taken in-- in the right line. But by the end of the selection, they decided to take that whole group, and they ended up in Treblinka. Later on, we found out. So my sisters never returned. And when my sisters did not return, and we didn't know their fate at the time, my mother and my father, they were so-- my mother was so upset she just left the bunker, and so did most able bodies. Except for us children and my grandmother and some people that didn't want to leave stayed on. We stayed until my brother saw fit to-- to get us out of there. Now that was a-- a story in itself, because [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] my-- they were telling people that they're going-- they're forming a Jewish state someplace in Madagascar or in Timbuktu, whatever they-- they told us. And my brother came, and he loaded us onto a wagon. And we were sitting on the wagon to go on, and by miracle, the last minute, he changed his mind and took us all off and smuggled us into the ghetto, which was a smaller ghetto which was right across the street from where-- where those wagons were loaded. I mean, just a half-- half a street to cross, like from one side of the street to the other, and we were already in the middle. And somehow he smuggled me into the ghetto, because it was not as hard for-- for adults to be going in, but for children-- and I think I was about seven at the time-- it was very difficult. Well, he got me into the ghetto. And for a while there, I I don't know if they knew what to do with me, the truth. And afterwards, people were rounded up to go to work, so he had to hide me. And I was hidden in a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in an attic where he lived, where my brother lived. My family was separated. My-- my mother and father were someplace else. I don't even know where they were at the time. But I was up in the attic, and my brother was with a lot of younger people, maybe policemen like him or-- he-- he wasn't with my parents. He wasn't with my-- with anybody. I don't even know where my little brother was. But I was in the attic and-- with another boy, who was the brother of another friend of my brother's who was also a policeman. And I stayed up there in the attic for a while. I don't know how long. And we used to be taken down every once in a while. But while I was up there, I had that problem of wetting. And my brother used to beg me every night, please don't wet. It's coming through the ceiling, and they're going to notice, and the-- the Germans are going to-- going to start looking around. And-- and-- and he used to make fun of me, and they used to call me a [POLISH], I-- which means a faucet, because that-- I must have really made marks on that ceiling. [LAUGHS] And they-- somehow we survived that, too. And I guess they used to bring us up food, because I really don't remember that part at all. I remember the other-- the other-- the other child was with me was a boy, also from, naturally, from my hometown, who-- who also went through the war, too. And when he got me down to the ghetto later on, and when I stopped being up in the attic, another selection came about. That was the second selection or the third or whatever it was. And he had to find me a new place, because he knew that the ghetto is going to be concentrated again or made smaller again or whatever it was.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he hid me and my-- my little brother together. And I don't remember where it was or what it was, but we were both hidden. And it was in the wintertime, because I remember after the selection was over the snow was on the ground when we came out of our hiding place.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remember the snow being all pink and red because there was so much blood on it. There were so-- so many killings. And everywhere you looked, everywhere you looked, the-- the-- the snow was all red. This is all I remember from-- from that selection. And after that, my grandmother was gone. My aunt was gone with her son and her husband. And I also found out-- actually, I found out recently that during that time, one of my aunts was hiding in her place, in her own home, with her three children and her husband. And the neighbors, the Poles, bludgeoned them with an ax, all five of them. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I just found that out. I thought they went through the camps with us, but they didn't. And we ended up in a little-- in a-- in a little ghetto, and it was like a half a street long from a big town of 16,000 Jews. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were-- I was a child, so I-- I didn't do nothing at the time yet [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] until I got to be in the camp. But somehow during that time, my brother got me involved with [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] some people that were going to the-- to the railroad tracks. They were working there and loading-- loading up-- because our town had a [POLISH], which is a brick factory. And we were loading bricks at-- at that-- at that-- on the loading docks. And I found that there were quite a few children. We were all cleaning up, like, used bricks, cleaning them up and putting them in stacks, and-- and that was what we did in the ghetto. And it was amazing that the Germans let us be at the time. But that, I guess, didn't last long, because the next step was another-- another selection into a concentration camp. And in that case, my brother came to us, to my little brother, who's five and a half years older than me, and he told us, we have to go somewhere by-- and get-- we have to survive by ourselves. He says, just leave this place and go. Just go.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't know where-- where do you go to survive? So we set out marching. And at the time, I probably did know more than I do now where these places were. And my brother knew a lot more than me, of course. So we went to the place where the people were working, which was-- we had a-- a steel factory in our town. It was an-- it was an industrial town. We had a steel factory and the brick factory. So there was a lot of work to be done for the Germans in our town. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So we set out to go to that brick factory, which was already defunct by that time, and it wasn't really working anymore. And my brother found us places where to hide. And the first place we hid was amongst hay, but it was [POLISH]. I don't know what you call [POLISH] in English.

speaker: interviewee
text: But it was not quite like bamboo, but it was bigger than hay. It was like thick-- thick stuff, and we hid in there. And every time you moved your bones, it would make a noise, and you could be detected. And it was like in an open barn-type place where these stacks were-- were placed, these stacks. And we went in between those stacks, and we pushed ourselves in to fit one of those stacks. That's what we did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's pick up with-- with that in a moment. We're going to change tapes.
